head_coach.name: Jeff Hafley
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Aggressive, attacking 4-3 defense with Wide-9 front and single-high safety structure; run-first, play-action-heavy offense; emphasizes physicality, toughness, and winning in the trenches

offensive_coordinator.name: Bobby Slowik
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Kyle Shanahan / West Coast wide zone

defensive_coordinator.name: Sean Duggan
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Saleh/Hafley 4-3 Wide-9 single-high; Carroll/Saleh lineage adapted through Hafley at SF, Ohio State, Boston College, Green Bay

offense.run_scheme: wide zone / outside zone primary, flexible to gap schemes based on personnel
offense.pass_concepts: Play-action heavy off zone run action, bootleg keepers, layered progressions, RPO slant concepts, vertical shots off play-action, screen game
offense.te_usage: Mixed — inline blocker in 12 personnel zone runs and play-action weapon; Shanahan tree values move TEs but Miami's TE room is thin; moderate flex/route usage expected
offense.rb_usage: Bell cow / centerpiece — De'Von Achane is the offensive focal point with 1,838 scrimmage yards in 2025; heavy dual-threat usage as both runner and receiver; committee backs Jaylen Wright and Ollie Gordon II spell him

defense.alignment: Even front (4-down), Wide-9 edge alignment, single-gap penetrating principles
defense.edge_rush_source: 4-3 DE hands-in-dirt, Wide-9 alignment (e.g., Chop Robinson, Bradley Chubb)
defense.coverage_base: Cover 3 primary (~36-38% of snaps), with Cover 1, inverted Cover 2, quarters, and Tampa-2 simulated pressures; predominantly zone (78%+ in Green Bay) with press-man mixed in on passing downs
defense.blitz_tendency: moderate overall (~27% pass blitz rate), but situationally aggressive — heavy blitz on 3rd and long (45%+ in GB); heavy use of simulated pressures (top-5 in NFL) to disguise rushers
defense.lb_role: Traditional 4-3 inside linebackers — MIKE and WILL handle run fits in single-gap scheme and zone drop responsibilities; speed and coverage ability prioritized over size; LBs do NOT serve as primary edge rushers
defense.safety_usage: Single-high free safety (post safety) patrols deep middle; strong safety/nickel hybrid plays box, slot, and robber roles; Minkah Fitzpatrick projected as versatile chess piece used in multiple alignments

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Slowik's Shanahan-tree offense features TEs as play-action targets and zone-run blockers in both 11 and 12 personnel. In Houston, Slowik used TEs like Dalton Schultz and Brevin Jordan in intermediate routes off play-action and in chip-and-release concepts. Hafley has specifically emphasized under-center play-action, bootlegs, and keepers — all of which create natural receiving opportunities for athletic TEs. However, Miami's TE room is thin and the offense is built around explosive WRs (Waddle) and RBs (Achane), so TEs are secondary receiving options, not primary weapons. A receiving TE gets moderate value — inline blocking and route versatility matter equally.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Hafley runs a 4-3 even front with four down linemen in Wide-9 alignment. Edge rush comes from hands-in-dirt 4-3 DEs (Chop Robinson, Bradley Chubb), NOT from stand-up OLBs. Inside linebackers in this scheme have single-gap run fit and zone coverage responsibilities — they are asked to play fast and cover ground, not rush the passer. Hafley's Green Bay defense was among the lowest in 5-man pressure usage (27th) and relied heavily on 4-man rush. LBs occasionally participate in simulated pressures on 3rd down (Quay Walker A-gap stunts), but this is situational, not a primary role. An LB with elite pass rush traits would be miscast as a starting ILB in this defense.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: De'Von Achane is the prototype: 1,838 scrimmage yards (1,350 rushing + 488 receiving) in 2025, functioning as Miami's offensive centerpiece. Slowik's Shanahan-tree offense inherently values backs who catch, run routes out of the backfield, and create mismatches against linebackers in space. Hafley explicitly wants play-action, screens, and bootleg concepts where RBs leak into the flat. With an uncertain QB situation (likely a young/new starter), the checkdown/screen role for the RB becomes even more critical. Any RB prospect with elite receiving traits, hands, and route-running ability fits this offense at a premium.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Hafley's defense is built on single-high safety structures that demand a versatile second safety/nickel hybrid. In Green Bay, Javon Bullard played 25% of his snaps in the slot and functioned as both a box safety and slot defender. Xavier McKinney was the deep post safety while Bullard/Evan Williams rotated through hybrid roles. PFF noted Minkah Fitzpatrick is 'exactly the kind of versatile chess piece Hafley adores' and projects him in multiple roles. Hafley's scheme uses inverted Cover-2, robber coverages, and slot coverage assignments that require safeties to play in the box, cover slot receivers man-to-man, and blitz. Any safety prospect with man coverage skills, tackling, and slot versatility gets a significant boost.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Miami's 2026 draft profile is shaped by two major forces: Slowik's Shanahan offense and Hafley's aggressive 4-3 Wide-9 defense. On OFFENSE: The scheme requires zone-blocking OL who can move laterally, a receiving-capable RB (already have Achane), and TEs who can both block in-line for zone runs and release into play-action routes. WR value is standard — speed helps in this deep-shot play-action system (Waddle fits). The RB dual-threat boost is among the highest in the NFL given Achane's role and the Shanahan tree's reliance on backs in the pass game. On DEFENSE: The 4-3 Wide-9 front means Miami values EDGE rushers who can win from wide alignments with speed-to-power — true DEs, not converted LBs. Interior DL must be single-gap penetrators (Kenneth Grant, Zach Sieler). ILBs must be fast, rangy, and coverage-capable — speed over size — not pass-rush LBs. The safety room is critical: Hafley's single-high/hybrid scheme creates premium value for safeties who can play in the slot, box, and deep. Minkah Fitzpatrick's versatility is the archetype. A safety prospect who can match up in man coverage against slot receivers while providing box support is valued at a premium here. LB-to-EDGE conversion is a NON-FACTOR in this scheme — unlike 3-4 teams, Miami's 4-3 provides no pathway for a linebacker to function as an edge rusher.
